Chemical compound

**Phenanthrene** is a [polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon](/wiki/Polycyclic_aromatic_hydrocarbon "Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon") (PAH) with formula C14H10, consisting of three fused [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene") rings. It is a colorless, crystal-like solid, but can also appear yellow. Phenanthrene is used to make dyes, plastics, pesticides, explosives, and drugs. It has also been used to make bile acids, cholesterol and steroids.[[3]](#cite_note-factsheet-3)

Phenanthrene occurs naturally and also is a man-made chemical. Commonly, humans are exposed to phenanthrene through inhalation of cigarette smoke, but there are many routes of exposure. Animal studies have shown that phenanthrene is a potential carcinogen.[[3]](#cite_note-factsheet-3) However, according to IARC, it is not identified as a probable, possible or confirmed human carcinogen.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Phenanthrene's three fused rings are angled as in the [phenacenes](/wiki/Phenacene "Phenacene"), rather than straight as in the [acenes](/wiki/Acene "Acene"). The compounds with a phenanthrene skeleton but with nitrogen atoms in place of CH sites are known as [phenanthrolines](/wiki/Phenanthroline "Phenanthroline").

## History and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and etymology")]

Phenanthrene was discovered in [coal tar](/wiki/Coal_tar "Coal tar") in 1872 independently by [Carl Graebe](/wiki/Carl_Graebe "Carl Graebe") (article manuscript received on November 1st[[5]](#cite_note-5)) as well as by [Wilhelm Rudolph Fittig](/wiki/Wilhelm_Rudolph_Fittig "Wilhelm Rudolph Fittig") and his doctoral student [Eugen Ostermayer](/w/index.php?title=Eugen_Ostermayer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eugen Ostermayer (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eugen_Ostermayer "de:Eugen Ostermayer")] (manuscript received on November 19th[[6]](#cite_note-6) but Ostermayer defended his dissertation in August[[7]](#cite_note-7)). Fittig and Ostermayer were able to determine the structure of the compound by oxidizing it first to a [corresponding quinone](/wiki/Phenanthrenequinone "Phenanthrenequinone") and then to [diphenic acid](/wiki/Diphenic_acid "Diphenic acid"), and soon Graebe confirmed it by a synthesis from [stilbene](/wiki/(Z)-Stilbene "(Z)-Stilbene").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Prior to February 1873 Fittig sent a letter to Graebe where he proposed to name the hydrocarbon phenanthrene ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Phenanthren*) in order to account for its similarity to [biphenyl](/wiki/Biphenyl "Biphenyl") and [anthracene](/wiki/Anthracene "Anthracene"), which was swiftly adopted.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Physical properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Physical properties")]

Phenanthrene is nearly insoluble in water but is soluble in most low-polarity organic solvents such as [toluene](/wiki/Toluene "Toluene"), [carbon tetrachloride](/wiki/Carbon_tetrachloride "Carbon tetrachloride"), [ether](/wiki/Diethyl_ether "Diethyl ether"), [chloroform](/wiki/Chloroform "Chloroform"), [acetic acid](/wiki/Acetic_acid "Acetic acid") and [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene").

Phenanthrene is fluorescent under ultraviolet light, exhibiting a large [Stoke shift](/wiki/Stokes_shift "Stokes shift").[[10]](#cite_note-10) It can be used in [scintillators](/wiki/Scintillator "Scintillator").

## Chemistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chemistry")]

Reactions of phenanthrene typically occur at the 9 and 10 positions, including:

* [Oxidation](/wiki/Organic_oxidation "Organic oxidation") with [chromic acid](/wiki/Chromic_acid "Chromic acid") gives [phenanthrenequinone](/wiki/Phenanthrenequinone "Phenanthrenequinone").[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [Organic reduction](/wiki/Organic_reduction "Organic reduction") to 9,10-dihydrophenanthrene with [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") gas and [raney nickel](/wiki/Raney_nickel "Raney nickel")[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Electrophilic halogenation](/wiki/Electrophilic_halogenation "Electrophilic halogenation") to 9-bromophenanthrene with [bromine](/wiki/Bromine "Bromine")[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Aromatic sulfonation](/wiki/Aromatic_sulfonation "Aromatic sulfonation") to 2 and 3-phenanthrenesulfonic acids with [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid")[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Ozonolysis](/wiki/Ozonolysis "Ozonolysis") to diphenylaldehyde[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Productions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Productions")]

Phenanthrene is extracted from coal tar, of which it comprises 5% by weight.[[16]](#cite_note-Ullmann-16)

In principle it could be obtained by chemical synthesis. The **Bardhan–Sengupta phenanthrene synthesis** is a classic way to make phenanthrenes.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

:   [![Bardhan–Senguptam phenanthrene synthesis](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Bardhan-SenguptaPhenanthreneSynthesis.png/500px-Bardhan-SenguptaPhenanthreneSynthesis.png)](/wiki/File:Bardhan-SenguptaPhenanthreneSynthesis.png "Bardhan–Senguptam phenanthrene synthesis")

This process involves [electrophilic aromatic substitution](/wiki/Electrophilic_aromatic_substitution "Electrophilic aromatic substitution") using a tethered [cyclohexanol](/wiki/Cyclohexanol "Cyclohexanol") group using [diphosphorus pentoxide](/wiki/Diphosphorus_pentoxide "Diphosphorus pentoxide"), which closes the central ring onto an existing aromatic ring. [Dehydrogenation](/wiki/Dehydrogenation "Dehydrogenation") using [selenium](/wiki/Selenium "Selenium") [aromatizes](/wiki/Aromatizes "Aromatizes") the other rings into aromatic ones as well. The aromatization of six-membered rings produces [H2Se](/wiki/Hydrogen_selenide "Hydrogen selenide").

Phenanthrene can also be obtained photochemically from certain [diarylethenes](/wiki/Diarylethene "Diarylethene") ([Mallory reaction](/wiki/Mallory_reaction "Mallory reaction")):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Scheme_7_Rev.png/250px-Scheme_7_Rev.png)](/wiki/File:Scheme_7_Rev.png)

Other synthesis routes include the [Haworth reaction](/wiki/Haworth_reaction "Haworth reaction") and the [Wagner-Meerwein](/wiki/Wagner-Meerwein_rearrangement "Wagner-Meerwein rearrangement")-type [ring-expansion](/wiki/Ring_expansion_and_contraction "Ring expansion and contraction"), as depicted below:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/StilAlt.png/250px-StilAlt.png)](/wiki/File:StilAlt.png)

Commercially phenanthrene is not synthesized but extracted from the byproducts of coal coking, since it makes around 4–6% of [coke oven](/wiki/Coke_oven "Coke oven") coal tar.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Natural occurrences of the phenanthrene derivatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Natural occurrences of the phenanthrene derivatives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Morphinan.svg/120px-Morphinan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Morphinan.svg)

Structure of morphinan, which features a partially reduced phenanthroline core

[Morphinan](/wiki/Morphinan "Morphinan") is the [chemical structure](/wiki/Chemical_structure "Chemical structure") found in several [psychoactive drugs](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug"), consisting of [opiate](/wiki/Opiate "Opiate") [analgesics](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic"), [cough suppressants](/wiki/Cough_suppressant "Cough suppressant"), and [dissociative](/wiki/Dissociative_drug "Dissociative drug") [hallucinogens](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen"), among others. Examples [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), and [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") (DXM).[[16]](#cite_note-Ullmann-16)

[Ravatite](/w/index.php?title=Ravatite&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ravatite (page does not exist)") is a natural mineral consisting of phenanthrene.[[19]](#cite_note-19) It is found in small amounts among a few coal burning sites. Ravatite represents a small group of organic minerals.

### In plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Phenanthrene&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In plants")]

Main article: [Phenanthrenes](/wiki/Phenanthrenes "Phenanthrenes")

Phenanthrene derivatives occur in plants as [phenanthrenoids](/wiki/Phenanthrenoid "Phenanthrenoid"). They have been reported from flowering plants, mainly in the family [Orchidaceae](/wiki/Orchidaceae "Orchidaceae"), and a few in the families [Dioscoreaceae](/wiki/Dioscoreaceae "Dioscoreaceae"), [Combretaceae](/wiki/Combretaceae "Combretaceae") and [Betulaceae](/wiki/Betulaceae "Betulaceae"), as well as in the lower plant class [Marchantiophyta](/wiki/Marchantiophyta "Marchantiophyta") (liverworts).[[20]](#cite_note-Kovacs-20)
